covered_sections: Scalars and Vectors>1.1 Scalars and Vectors
Vector addition and subtraction>1.3 Vector addition and subtraction

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a scalar quantity?

options.A: A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
options.B: A quantity that has only magnitude and no direction
options.C: A quantity that has only direction and no magnitude
options.D: A quantity that changes with time

questions.hint: Consider which type of physical quantity is fully described by a number and unit alone.
questions.explanation: A scalar quantity is defined as having only magnitude, as seen with mass or temperature. Option A incorrectly describes a vector, which requires both magnitude and direction for complete description.

source_trace.content_anchor: Scalars are quantities that has only magnitude

explanations_tiered.definition: A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (a number and unit) alone.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Scalars are the simplest type of physical quantity. They do not involve direction, making them easier to add and compare than vectors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a scalar like the temperature of a room: 25°C tells you everything. You don't need to say which way the temperature is pointing.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A scalar is fully defined by magnitude only. Common examples include mass (50 kg), time (10 s), and distance (100 m). No direction is required.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalars are rank-0 tensors that remain invariant under coordinate rotations. Mathematically, they belong to the field \(\mathbb{R}\) and obey ordinary arithmetic rules, unlike vectors which require directional algebra.
reasoning_steps: Identify what information is required to describe the quantity completely.
Check if direction is needed.
If only magnitude is needed, it is a scalar.
If both magnitude and direction are needed, it is a vector.
misconceptions: Some students believe scalars cannot be negative. Negative scalars exist (e.g., -10°C), but direction is still not implied.
Scalars are sometimes confused with vector magnitudes, but magnitude is always non-negative while scalars can be negative in certain contexts.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scalar: magnitude only. Vector: magnitude + direction.

glossary_entry.definition: A physical quantity that has only magnitude and no direction.

flashcard.back: A scalar quantity has only magnitude and no direction. Examples: mass, time, temperature.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Only magnitude, no direction. Example: mass = 50 kg.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude alone, without any directional information.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Scalar: magnitude only. Vector: magnitude + direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students think scalars cannot be negative. Use temperature (-5°C) to counter this.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between scalar and vector quantities
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. A scalar quantity is defined only by its magnitude, unlike vectors which require direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which physical quantity requires both a numerical value and a direction for complete description?
questions.hint: Think about which measurement becomes ambiguous without a specified direction.
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity requiring both magnitude (20 m/s) and direction (north) for complete description. Option A (mass) and D (time) are scalars; B (temperature) is also a scalar.

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector quantity is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction and follows vector addition rules.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vectors require directional information. Without direction, the description is incomplete, as seen with displacement vs. distance.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine saying 'the car is moving at 60 km/h' — that's scalar speed. Adding 'north' makes it vector velocity, giving full information.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vector quantities like velocity, force, and displacement include directional information. Saying '50 N' is incomplete; '50 N upward' is a complete vector description.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vectors transform under coordinate rotations via \(V'_i = R_{ij} V_j\). This directional dependence distinguishes them from scalars, which are rotationally invariant.
reasoning_steps: Identify if direction is stated or implied.
Check if the quantity is fully described without direction.
If direction is required, the quantity is a vector.
Select the option that includes both magnitude and direction.
misconceptions: Students often think speed is a vector because it relates to motion. Speed is scalar (magnitude only); velocity is the vector.
Some believe all measured numbers with signs (e.g., -10 m) are vectors. Negative scalars exist without implying direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Vector = magnitude + direction. Scalar = magnitude only.

glossary_entry.definition: A physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction, and follows the laws of vector addition.

flashcard.front: What two things define a vector quantity?
flashcard.back: Magnitude and direction. Example: \(\vec{F} = 10\text{ N upward}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does a vector differ from a scalar?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A vector has magnitude AND direction; a scalar has only magnitude.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A physical quantity characterized by both magnitude and direction, such as velocity or force.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Vector: magnitude + direction. Example: \(20\text{ m/s north}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a compass or map to illustrate why direction matters for vectors like displacement.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between scalar and vector quantities and give examples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Velocity includes direction (north), making it a vector. Mass, temperature, and time are scalars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: How is a vector represented algebraically?

options.A: As a plain italic letter like A
options.B: As a bold letter like \(\mathbf{A}\) or with an arrow \(\vec{A}\)
options.C: As a number with a plus sign

questions.hint: Think about typographical conventions used in textbooks to distinguish vectors from scalars.
questions.explanation: Vectors are algebraically represented by boldface letters (\(\mathbf{A}\)) or letters with an arrow (\(\vec{A}\)). Option A is incorrect because plain italics usually denote scalars.

source_trace.content_anchor: Algebraically, they are represented by a bold letter as A or with an arrow over the letter

explanations_tiered.definition: Algebraic vector representation uses boldface or arrow notation to distinguish vectors from scalar quantities.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Notation matters in physics: \(\vec{A}\) or \(\mathbf{A}\) means vector; \(A\) usually means magnitude or scalar.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a vector like an address: you need a street (magnitude) AND a city (direction). Bold or arrow notation is just a way to show both are included.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vectors are written as \(\vec{v}\) or \(\mathbf{v}\) to indicate they have direction. The magnitude is written as \(v = |\vec{v}|\), which is a scalar.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In tensor notation, vectors are rank-1 tensors denoted by indices (\(v^i\)), but in elementary physics, bold \(\mathbf{v}\) or \(\vec{v}\) is standard to emphasize directional properties.
reasoning_steps: Recall that scalars use plain or italic letters.
Recall that vectors use special notation.
Identify bold or arrow notation as vector-specific.
Select the option matching vector notation.
misconceptions: Some students think any variable with a letter is a vector. Only bold or arrow notation indicates a vector.
The same letter can represent both vector and scalar (e.g., \(\vec{v}\) = velocity vector, \(v\) = speed).

glossary_entry.definition: The convention of using boldface or an arrow over a letter to denote a vector quantity.

flashcard.front: How do you write a vector algebraically?
flashcard.back: As a bold letter \(\mathbf{A}\) or with an arrow \(\vec{A}\). Example: \(\vec{v} = 20\text{ m/s}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What notation indicates a vector in algebra?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The use of boldface or arrow notation to distinguish vectors from scalars.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that \(\vec{A}\) and \(A\) are different: one is vector, one is scalar magnitude.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Demonstrate vectors representation graphically and algebraically
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Vectors are algebraically shown as bold or with an arrow. Option A is scalar notation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A vector is drawn to scale with a length of 5 cm representing a displacement of 20 km. What is the scale used?
questions.hint: Divide the actual displacement by the drawn length to find km per cm.
questions.explanation: Scale = actual magnitude / drawn length = \(20\text{ km} / 5\text{ cm} = 4\text{ km/cm}\). Option A (2 km/cm) would give 10 km total, not 20 km.

source_trace.content_anchor: graphically draw the following numerical vectors

explanations_tiered.definition: Scale in vector diagrams is the ratio between the drawn length and the actual magnitude of the quantity represented.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A scale converts measured lengths on paper to real-world magnitudes, allowing graphical representation of physical quantities.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If 1 cm on a map means 1 km in real life, that's the scale. Here, 5 cm drawn = 20 km real, so each cm is 4 km.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Scale \(S = \frac{\text{actual magnitude}}{\text{drawn length}}\). With \(L_{\text{drawn}} = 5\text{ cm}\) and \(R_{\text{actual}} = 20\text{ km}\), \(S = 20/5 = 4\text{ km/cm}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scale choice affects vector diagram accuracy. With \(S = 4\text{ km/cm}\), the scale factor is \(4 \times 10^3\text{ m} / 0.01\text{ m} = 4 \times 10^5\), a dimensionless ratio of \(4 \times 10^5:1\).
reasoning_steps: Identify actual magnitude = 20 km.
Identify drawn length = 5 cm.
Divide actual by drawn: \(20/5 = 4\).
Express as '1 cm = X km' where X = 4.
misconceptions: Some students invert the scale (5/20 = 0.25) instead of 20/5 = 4.
Units must match: if actual is in km and drawn in cm, the scale is km/cm.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scale = actual / drawn. 20 km / 5 cm = 4 km/cm.

glossary_entry.definition: The ratio between the drawn length of a vector and its actual magnitude.

flashcard.front: How do you find scale from drawn length and actual magnitude?
flashcard.back: Scale = actual magnitude / drawn length. Example: 20 km / 5 cm = 4 km/cm.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A 5 cm drawn vector = 20 km actual. What is the scale?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The ratio linking drawn length to actual magnitude in vector diagrams.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Scale = actual ÷ drawn. 20 km ÷ 5 cm = 4 km/cm.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students practice converting between scale, drawn length, and actual magnitude.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Scale = actual/drawn = 20 km / 5 cm = 4 km/cm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the term for two vectors that have the same magnitude but point in opposite directions?
questions.hint: One vector is the additive inverse of the other.
questions.explanation: Two vectors with equal magnitude but opposite direction are called negative vectors, where \(\vec{B} = -\vec{A}\). Option A describes same direction; B describes same or opposite direction; D describes 90° separation.

explanations_tiered.definition: A negative vector has the same magnitude as the original vector but points in the opposite direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The negative of a vector reverses its direction while preserving magnitude. This is essential for vector subtraction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 10 m east (+10 m east), walking 10 m west is the negative vector. You end up back where you started.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If \(\vec{A}\) points east with magnitude 5 N, then \(-\vec{A}\) points west with magnitude 5 N. Adding them gives zero: \(\vec{A} + (-\vec{A}) = 0\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Negation of a vector is equivalent to multiplying by scalar -1: \(-\vec{A} = (-1)\vec{A}\). This reverses each component: if \(\vec{A} = (A_x, A_y)\), then \(-\vec{A} = (-A_x, -A_y)\).
reasoning_steps: Check if magnitudes are equal.
Check if directions are opposite.
If both true, one is the negative of the other.
Select 'negative vectors'.
misconceptions: Negative vector does not mean 'bad' or 'less than zero' magnitude. It means opposite direction.
Some think \(-\vec{A}\) has negative magnitude. Magnitude is always positive or zero; direction changes sign.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Negative vector: same magnitude, opposite direction: \(\vec{B} = -\vec{A}\).

glossary_entry.definition: A vector with the same magnitude as another vector but pointing in the exact opposite direction.

flashcard.back: Same magnitude as original, opposite direction. \(\vec{B} = -\vec{A}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What do you call a vector with same magnitude but opposite direction?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A vector equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to a given vector.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use position on a number line: +5 and -5 as one-dimensional analog to negative vectors.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Understand vector properties including negative vectors
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Negative vectors have equal magnitude but opposite direction. Option A requires same direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two forces act on an object: \(\vec{F}_1 = 6\text{ N}\) east and \(\vec{F}_2 = 3\text{ N}\) west. What is the magnitude of the resultant force?
questions.hint: For opposite directions, subtract the smaller magnitude from the larger.
questions.explanation: Since the forces are opposite (east vs west), the resultant magnitude is \(|6 - 3| = 3\text{ N}\). Option A (9 N) would be correct only if both acted in the same direction.

calculation_steps: Identify forces: \(F_1 = 6\text{ N}\) east, \(F_2 = 3\text{ N}\) west.
Since opposite direction, resultant magnitude = \(|F_1 - F_2|\).

source_trace.content_anchor: When the two vectors are in opposite directions, R = (6 - 3) units = 3 units

explanations_tiered.definition: The resultant of two antiparallel vectors is the difference of their magnitudes, directed along the larger vector.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When vectors point in exactly opposite directions, the resultant magnitude equals the absolute difference of magnitudes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine two people pulling a rope: one east with 6 N, one west with 3 N. The net pull is 3 N east — the stronger side minus the weaker.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(\vec{F}_1\) east and \(\vec{F}_2\) west, take east as positive. \(F_1 = +6\) N, \(F_2 = -3\) N. Resultant = \(+6 + (-3) = +3\) N (3 N east).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The vector sum is \(\vec{R} = \vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2\). With opposite directions, \(|\vec{R}| = |F_1 - F_2|\) when \(F_1 \ge F_2\). Direction follows the larger vector.
worked_solution: Step 1: Assign east as positive direction.
reasoning_steps: Identify direction of each vector.
Set a sign convention (e.g., east positive, west negative).
Add vectors algebraically.
Take absolute value for magnitude if only magnitude is asked.
misconceptions: Students often add magnitudes (6+3=9 N) even when directions are opposite.
Some forget that resultant direction matters, but magnitude only is asked here.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Opposite vectors: subtract magnitudes. Same direction: add magnitudes.

glossary_entry.definition: The magnitude equals the absolute difference of the two magnitudes, direction same as larger vector.

flashcard.front: How do you find resultant magnitude of two opposite vectors?
flashcard.back: Subtract smaller magnitude from larger. \(R = |A - B|\). Example: 6 N east + 3 N west = 3 N east.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 3 N east. Opposite directions: subtract magnitudes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The single vector that produces the same effect as the sum of multiple vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use number line: +6 and -3 sum to +3 to reinforce sign convention.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Opposite directions: subtract magnitudes = 6-3=3 N. Option A is for same direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Under the condition that two vectors are perpendicular, which method correctly gives the magnitude of their resultant?
questions.hint: Consider the geometric relationship between perpendicular sides of a right triangle.
questions.explanation: For perpendicular vectors, the resultant forms the hypotenuse of a right triangle, so \(R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\). Option A works only for parallel same-direction vectors; B works for opposite directions.

source_trace.content_anchor: When the two vectors are perpendicular, R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2)

explanations_tiered.definition: For two perpendicular vectors, the resultant magnitude is the square root of the sum of squares of the magnitudes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The resultant of perpendicular vectors is the hypotenuse of a right triangle, following the Pythagorean theorem.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 3 m east then 4 m north, you end up 5 m from start. That's \(\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = 5\) m — perpendicular vector addition.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: When \(\vec{A} \perp \vec{B}\), they form legs of a right triangle. The resultant \(\vec{R}\) is the hypotenuse: \(R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\). Direction given by \(\tan\theta = B/A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For orthogonal vectors, the dot product \(\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 0\). Then \(|\vec{R}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 + 2\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = A^2 + B^2\), so \(R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify vectors are perpendicular (90° apart).
Step 3: Compute squares, add, take square root.
reasoning_steps: Check if vectors are perpendicular (90°).
Recall right triangle relationship.
Apply \(R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\).
Compute numeric value if magnitudes given.
misconceptions: Students mistakenly add magnitudes for perpendicular vectors (e.g., 3+4=7 not 5).
Some think resultant of perpendicular vectors is always larger than either, which is true but not the correct method.

glossary_entry.definition: The resultant magnitude of two perpendicular vectors given by the Pythagorean theorem.

flashcard.front: How do you find resultant magnitude of two perpendicular vectors?
flashcard.back: Use Pythagorean theorem: \(R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\). Example: 3 N east + 4 N north = 5 N.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for resultant of perpendicular vectors?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The rule that the resultant magnitude of two perpendicular vectors equals the square root of the sum of squares.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a right triangle on board to show why Pythagorean theorem applies.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Add two vectors using analytical methods
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Perpendicular vectors follow Pythagorean theorem. Option A is for same direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A displacement vector \(\vec{S}\) has magnitude \(250\text{ km}\) at \(30^\circ\) north of east. What is the eastward component \(S_E\)?
questions.hint: Use cosine for the adjacent side (east component).
questions.explanation: The east component is \(S_E = S\cos\theta = 250 \times \cos 30^\circ = 250 \times 0.866 = 216.5\text{ km}\). Option A (125 km) uses sine, which gives the north component.

explanations_tiered.definition: The component of a vector along a direction is the projection of the vector onto that axis, given by magnitude times cosine of the included angle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vector resolution uses trigonometry: horizontal component = magnitude × cos(angle from horizontal); vertical component = magnitude × sin(angle from horizontal).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a 250 km arrow pointing 30° above east. The east part is like the shadow on the ground: \(250 \times \cos 30^\circ = 216.5\) km east.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For vector \(\vec{S}\) at angle \(\theta\) from east, \(S_x = S\cos\theta\) and \(S_y = S\sin\theta\). Here \(S_x = 250\cos 30^\circ = 216.5\) km east.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In coordinate form, \(\vec{S} = (S\cos\theta, S\sin\theta)\). The east component \(S_x\) is the scalar projection onto the x-axis: \(S_x = |\vec{S}|\cos\theta\).
reasoning_steps: Determine which component is needed (east = horizontal).
Use cosine for adjacent side to the angle.
Multiply magnitude by cosine of angle.
Compute numeric result.
misconceptions: Students often confuse sine and cosine: sine gives opposite side (north), cosine gives adjacent (east).
Using degrees vs radians incorrectly — here degrees are correct as given.

glossary_entry.definition: The projection of a vector onto a coordinate axis, found using trigonometric functions.

flashcard.front: How do you find east component of a vector at angle \(\theta\) north of east?
flashcard.back: East component = magnitude × cos(\(\theta\)). Example: 250 km at 30° gives 216.5 km east.

visual_system.visual_description: A right triangle with hypotenuse representing vector S of length 250 km at 30° above the horizontal (east) axis. The horizontal leg is the east component (S_E = 216.5 km), vertical leg is the north component (S_N = 125 km).
visual_system.caption: Figure: Resolution of a displacement vector into east and north components.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Right triangle diagram. Hypotenuse labeled 250 km at 30 degrees above horizontal. Horizontal leg labeled S_E = 216.5 km east. Vertical leg labeled S_N = 125 km north.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for horizontal component of a vector?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: \(A_x = A\cos\theta\). Example: 250 km at 30° gives 216.5 km east.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The process of breaking a vector into perpendicular components along chosen axes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a right triangle on board: hypotenuse vector, legs as components.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Resolve a vector into horizontal and vertical components
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. East component = magnitude × cos(30°) = 250 × 0.866 = 216.5 km.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why can a vector never have a component greater than its own magnitude?

options.B: Because components are perpendicular projections, and \(\cos\theta \leq 1\) or \(\sin\theta \leq 1\)
options.C: Because magnitude is the sum of components

questions.hint: Recall the trigonometric factors for components: cosine and sine range from -1 to 1.
questions.explanation: Components are \(A_x = A\cos\theta\) and \(A_y = A\sin\theta\), and \(|\cos\theta| \leq 1\), \(|\sin\theta| \leq 1\). Thus \(|A_x| \leq A\) and \(|A_y| \leq A\). Option C is false; magnitude is \(\sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2}\), not the sum.

source_trace.content_anchor: Explain why a vector cannot have a component greater than its own magnitude

explanations_tiered.definition: A vector component is the projection of the vector onto an axis, found by multiplying the magnitude by the cosine or sine of the angle relative to that axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since cosine and sine are bounded between -1 and 1, the component magnitude can never exceed the vector's magnitude.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a shadow: a stick's shadow is never longer than the stick itself. The component is like the shadow on the ground.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Component = magnitude × trigonometric factor. \(|\cos\theta| \le 1\) and \(|\sin\theta| \le 1\), so \(|A_x| \le A\) and \(|A_y| \le A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The component \(A_x = |\vec{A}|\cos\theta\) satisfies \(|A_x| = |\vec{A}||\cos\theta| \le |\vec{A}|\) because \(|\cos\theta| \le 1\). Equality occurs only when \(\theta = 0^\circ\) or \(180^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Recall component formulas: \(A_x = A\cos\theta\), \(A_y = A\sin\theta\).
Recall range of cosine and sine: \(-1 \le \cos\theta \le 1\), \(-1 \le \sin\theta \le 1\).
Multiply by magnitude \(A\): \(-A \le A_x \le A\), \(-A \le A_y \le A\).
Thus absolute component ≤ magnitude.

formula_analysis.name: Component formula
formula_analysis.derivation: From definition of cosine in right triangle

misconceptions: Some think components can be larger than the vector if the vector points diagonally, but that violates triangle inequality.
Students confuse component magnitude with vector magnitude — the component is a projection, not a separate vector.

glossary_entry.definition: The magnitude of a vector component cannot exceed the magnitude of the original vector.

flashcard.front: Can a vector component ever be larger than the vector's magnitude? Why?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Because Ax = A cos θ and |cos θ| ≤ 1, so |Ax| ≤ A.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The principle that a vector's component cannot exceed its magnitude due to trigonometric bounds.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a stick and its shadow on the ground and wall to illustrate.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Components are \(A\cos\theta\) or \(A\sin\theta\), and \(\cos\theta, \sin\theta \leq 1\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the head-to-tail (triangle) method of vector addition, where is the tail of the second vector placed?

options.A: At the tail of the first vector
options.B: At the head of the first vector

questions.explanation: In the triangle method, the tail of the second vector is placed at the head (tip) of the first vector. The resultant joins the tail of first to head of last. Option A describes the parallelogram method, not triangle method.

source_trace.content_anchor: Draw vector B to the same scale with its tail at the tip of A

explanations_tiered.definition: The head-to-tail (triangle) method places the tail of each subsequent vector at the head of the previous vector to find the resultant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This graphical method chains vectors by connecting the head of one to the tail of the next, forming a path.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine walking: take 3 steps east (head), then from there take 4 steps north (tail at head). Your straight-line path home is the resultant.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In triangle method: first vector \(\vec{A}\) drawn; then \(\vec{B}\) placed with its tail at \(\vec{A}\)'s head. Resultant \(\vec{R}\) goes from tail of \(\vec{A}\) to head of \(\vec{B}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: This method relies on vector addition being associative: \(\vec{A} + \vec{B} = \vec{R}\). Graphically, the triangle closes when \(\vec{R}\) is drawn from start to finish.
reasoning_steps: Draw first vector from origin.
Place second vector's tail at first vector's head.
Draw second vector with correct magnitude and direction.
Draw resultant from origin (tail of first) to head of second.
misconceptions: Some place both tails together (parallelogram method) for triangle method.
Students may draw resultant incorrectly from head of first to tail of second.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Head-to-tail: tail of next at head of previous.

glossary_entry.definition: A graphical method where vectors are placed sequentially with the tail of each at the head of the previous, and the resultant connects first tail to last head.

flashcard.front: Where is the second vector's tail placed in head-to-tail method?
flashcard.back: At the head (tip) of the first vector.

visual_system.visual_description: Two vectors: A (horizontal right) then B (upward). Tail of B at head of A. Resultant R drawn from tail of A to head of B forming triangle.
visual_system.caption: Figure: Triangle method of vector addition.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Diagram: Vector A horizontal right. Vector B vertical up starting at tip of A. Resultant R from start of A to tip of B.

labels.text: Head of A / Tail of B

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you place vectors in triangle method?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Tail of second vector at head of first (head-to-tail).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A graphical method adding two vectors by placing them head-to-tail to form a triangle.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Head-to-tail: tail of next at head of previous.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use physical arrows on board to demonstrate head-to-tail placement.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the graphical method of vector addition
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Head-to-tail means second vector's tail at first vector's head.
